one_line_takeaway: ShaPO shows that restricting SAM-style worst-case parameter perturbations to a small probe-identified safety-critical neuron subspace (rather than applying uniform sharpness-aware regularization) yields lower attack success rates than data-centric robust-DPO baselines under domain shift and noisy preference supervision, and composes additively with those data-centric methods.
key_finding: Selective (probe-guided, Top-1%) geometry control outperforms both no control and uniform (all-parameter) geometry control, and substantially outperforms random-neuron control, especially under OOD evaluation: on LLaMA-3-8B with DPO loss (Table 3), OOD average ASR is 28.78%/16.91% (MD/NV) with no control vs. 26.83%/15.12% with uniform control vs. 26.31%/14.12% with selective control, while IID ASR is roughly comparable across strategies (26.57%-27.32% MD), indicating the benefit of selectivity is concentrated in the distribution-shift regime rather than in-distribution. Under domain shift (Table 2), ShaPO-T and ShaPO-R achieve the lowest average ASR across four OOD benchmarks on both LLaMA-3-8B (ShaPO-T: 1.37% avg vs. Dr.DPO 2.09%, rDPO 3.97%, DPO 22.85%) and Qwen2.5-7B (ShaPO-T: 0.42% avg vs. Dr.DPO 1.05%, DPO 14.39%), with ShaPO-R further improving to 0.70% and 0.55% average ASR respectively. Under noisy supervision (Figure 3), ShaPO-R maintains the highest win rate across all flip rates up to 40%, attributed to its use of an external, label-decoupled reward model as supervision. The paper's own controlled ablation (Table 5) shows that under DPO, adding reward supervision alone (DPO+Reward) already yields large gains, but ShaPO-R further reduces ASR beyond DPO+Reward (e.g., IID MD 26.57% DPO -> 18.42% DPO+Reward -> 16.32% ShaPO-R); under Dr.DPO, reward supervision alone gives only marginal gains (Dr.DPO 4.39%/0.50% -> Dr.DPO+Reward 3.38%/0.38% IID MD/NV) yet ShaPO-R still achieves further improvement (1.13%/0.13%), which the authors use to argue the robustness gain is attributable to optimization geometry, not merely to the added reward signal. A direct geometry measurement (Table 4) shows maximum Hessian eigenvalue along the safety subspace decreasing monotonically from 25.2336 (Base) to 14.9509 (DPO) to 9.4993 (Dr.DPO) to 6.2466 (ShaPO-T) on LLaMA-3-8B, correlating with win rate increasing from 42.82% to 85.45%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ShaPO (Sharpness-aware Preference Optimization), a geometry-aware robust alignment framework for LLM safety that restricts worst-case (sharpness-aware) parameter perturbations during preference optimization to a small 'safety-critical' parameter subspace, rather than applying uniform robustness regularization across all parameters. The safety-critical subspace is identified by training a linear probe on final-layer residual-stream representations to separate safe vs. unsafe responses, then selecting the Top-K value vectors in transformer blocks whose similarity to the probe direction is highest (the paper conservatively uses Top-1% of neurons, which they show empirically covers >90% of the worst-case alignment loss increase, cf. their Figure 1 motivating study). ShaPO is instantiated at two levels: (1) token-level ShaPO, which applies min-max sharpness-aware optimization (SAM-style, two forward-backward passes with perturbation restricted to the selected subspace) directly to a DPO-style token-likelihood loss (Eq. 8); and (2) reward-level ShaPO, which instead applies selective geometry control to a binary cross-entropy loss trained against soft preference targets derived from a pretrained external reward/cost model (PKU-Alignment/beaver-7b-v1.0-cost) (Eq. 9-12). The authors argue and empirically support that robustness failures in safety alignment are not solely explained by data/annotation uncertainty (the focus of prior data-centric robust-DPO work such as IPO, cDPO, rDPO, Dr.DPO) but also by 'optimization-induced fragility,' i.e., sharp loss landscapes around safety-critical parameter directions that amplify small data or perturbation noise, and that ShaPO is complementary/composable with data-centric methods.
threat_model: This is a robustness/alignment-quality paper, not an adversarial-attacker security paper in the classical sense: the 'threat' is (a) distribution shift between the training safety-preference dataset (PKU-SafeRLHF-30K) and deployment/evaluation safety benchmarks (Do-Not-Answer, HarmBench, HH-RLHF-Safety, SaladBench), and (b) noisy/corrupted preference supervision, operationalized as controlled label flips (0-40% flipped preference pairs) injected into training data while evaluation is done on clean validation sets. There is no adaptive/white-box adversary crafting jailbreak prompts against the trained model in this paper's own experiments beyond the pre-existing benchmark prompts (HarmBench, SaladBench, etc.) used as fixed evaluation sets; safety is measured via automated LLM-judge Attack Success Rate (ASR, using MD-Judge-v0_2-internlm2_7b and NVIDIA Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Safety-Guard-8B-v3) and human/judge-based Win Rate, not by the authors mounting novel attacks.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion): ShaPO introduces additional computational overhead from geometry-aware optimization (two forward-backward passes for the sharpness-aware step), and the authors flag improving efficiency/scalability as future work, particularly for larger-scale models -- the largest backbone evaluated is 8B parameters. The identification of alignment-critical directions is explicitly described as based on 'approximate' probe-derived signals rather than a provably correct localization. Additional limitations evident from the methodology as described: the safety-critical subspace is derived from a single linear probe trained on final-layer residual-stream activations, so its fidelity as a proxy for 'true' safety-relevant parameters is not independently validated beyond the Top-K vs Random-K loss-contribution comparison in Figure 1; reward-level ShaPO depends on a single external safety reward/cost model (PKU-Alignment/beaver-7b-v1.0-cost), so its robustness gains are partly contingent on that model's own quality and could inherit its biases or blind spots (the paper does not test alternative reward models); robustness evaluation uses automated LLM-judge ASR scoring (MD-Judge, NVIDIA Nemotron-Safety-Guard) rather than human evaluation, which is a judge-model-dependent proxy; and the noisy-supervision experiments in Figure 3 are reported only for Pythia-2.8B and LLaMA-3.2-3B backbones (not the larger LLaMA-3-8B/Qwen2.5-7B used elsewhere), so the noise-robustness claims are not shown at the larger scales in the main figure.

quantitative_results: IID (Table 1), LLaMA-3-8B: DPO WinRate 69.19%/MD-ASR 26.57%/NV-ASR 14.41% vs ShaPO-T 85.45%/2.76%/0.13% vs ShaPO-R 87.45%/1.13%/0.13%
OOD average ASR (Table 2), LLaMA-3-8B: DPO 22.85% avg vs Dr.DPO 2.09% avg vs ShaPO-T 1.37% avg vs ShaPO-R 0.70% avg
OOD average ASR (Table 2), Qwen2.5-7B: DPO 14.39% avg vs Dr.DPO 1.05% avg vs ShaPO-T 0.42% avg vs ShaPO-R 0.55% avg
Geometry-control ablation on LLaMA-3-8B (Table 3), OOD avg: No Control 28.78%/16.91% (MD/NV) vs Uniform Control 26.83%/15.12% vs Selective Control 26.31%/14.12%
Max Hessian Eigenvalue vs Win Rate on LLaMA-3-8B (Table 4): Base 25.2336/42.82%, DPO 14.9509/69.19%, Dr.DPO 9.4993/82.50%, ShaPO-T 6.2466/85.45%
Reward-supervision ablation (Table 5), IID MD-ASR on LLaMA-3-8B: DPO 26.57% -> DPO+Reward 18.42% -> ShaPO-R 16.32%; Dr.DPO 4.39% -> Dr.DPO+Reward 3.38% -> ShaPO-R 1.13%

Composability gains in Win Rate when adding ShaPO to baselines on Pythia-2.8B: +1.36% (DPO), +1.47% (IPO), +2.01% (cDPO), +2.23% (rDPO), +3.48% (Dr.DPO); on LLaMA-3.2-3B: +1.92% (DPO), +1.52% (IPO), +2.22% (cDPO), +1.95% (rDPO), +2.11% (Dr.DPO)
Motivating study (Figure 1): perturbing fewer than 0.5% of probe-identified safety-critical neurons accounts for over 80% of cumulative worst-case alignment loss increase
